raw_case:
Pregnancy-related decidualization in post-cesarean section scar endometriosis: A case report


Introduction and importance

Post-cesarean section decidualized endometriosis is a rare phenomenon where ectopic endometrial cells undergo changes, such as atypia, under the influence of high progesterone levels at the site of cesarean section. This phenomenon can create a diagnostic challenge as it mimics the characteristics of malignancy to some extent.

Case presentation

We report a case of a 38-year-old G6P2 woman who was electively referred to the OB ward for a cesarean section. Ultrasonography revealed a 38 × 29 × 26 mm lobulated solid lesion within the rectus abdominis muscle located in the midline of the suprapubic abdominal wall in the 16th week of gestation. Histological evaluation of the specimen showed endometrial glands surrounded by severely decidualized stroma.

Clinical discussion

To our knowledge, we present the ninth case report of decidualized endometriosis located on a post-cesarean section scar. Our case was in the younger demographics and histopathologically her resected tissue contained glandular structures. There was no past medical history of endometriosis. Extra pelvic localization of the tissue could have led us to a diagnostic pitfall.

Conclusion

Understanding this phenomenon's pathologic and clinical characteristics can help pathologists and surgeons to rule out malignancy and avoid conspicuous diagnostic errors.

Highlights

•Post-cesarean section decidualized endometriosis is a rare phenomenon.•This phenomenon can create a diagnostic challenge.•It mimics the characteristics of malignancy to some extent.•Understanding pathologic and clinical characteristics can help to avoid conspicuous diagnostic errors.1

Introduction

Endometriosis is defined as the presence of the functional layer of the endometrium outside the uterus [1]. 10 % of the women within the reproductive age would experience endometriosis [2]. Endometriosis is frequently located in organs adjacent to the uterus namely the cervix, vagina, vulva, rectovaginal septum, ovary, fallopian tubes, uterine ligaments, appendix, small and large bowel, bladder and ureters, pelvic peritoneum, etc [1] However, the location of endometriosis is not confined to adjacent sites, extending to remote areas such as the lung, pleura, peripheral nerves, and skin. Despite its rarity, post-cesarean section endometriosis is a common occurrence among endometriosis caused by surgical scars [1,3]. It is believed that this disease is caused by the iatrogenic deposition of tissue in the surgical scar [3].As endometriosis is subordinate to hormonal changes, it can potentially mimic the changes in the endometrium via conception or exogenic estrogen consumption. There are several case reports describing decidualization in post-cesarean section endometriosis [[4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], [11]]. In these prior reports, the issue was that this condition may be misinterpreted as malignancy due to its location and atypia of the cells [4,6]. Herein, we report the 9th case of pregnancy-related decidualization in post-cesarean section scar endometriosis [[4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], [11]].To the best of our knowledge, few cases of pregnancy-related decidualization in post-cesarean section endometriosis have been reported so far. There is a lack of evidence regarding this phenomenon. We thereupon present the 9th case of pregnancy-related decidualization in post-cesarean section endometriosis.2

Case report

In July 2021 a 38-year-old woman, with a known case of hypothyroidism, was electively referred to the OB ward in Fasa hospital for a cesarean section. The reason for choosing cesarean section over vaginal delivery was prior cesarean section. The patient was G6P2 at the time of admission, two of the gravidities being abortion and one of which being molar pregnancy. The intended offspring's gestational age was 38 weeks and 1 day.There was neither clinical nor paraclinical clue for endometriosis until the 16th week of pregnancy when a routine OB, NT/NB, uterus, and ovaries ultrasonography was performed for the patient. In this modality, the radiologist observed a 38 × 29 × 26 mm lobulated solid lesion within the rectus abdominis muscle located in the midline of the suprapubic abdominal wall. The radiologist's primary differential diagnosis for this lesion was scar endometriosis.During hospitalization, laboratory studies were performed. The patient showed normocytic anemia (MCV: 83.5, Hb:10.1) and mild hypokalemia (K: 3.3). Cesarean section was performed for the patient under spinal anesthesia; the abdominal wall was opened by a Pfannenstiel incision, and the lesion which was revealed by ultrasonography, was resected.Macroscopic evaluation of the lesion revealed fibrofatty tissue measuring 40 × 20 × 5 mm which displayed a homogenous whitish surface on the serial cut sections. No local hemorrhage was seen. Histological evaluation of the specimen showed endometrial glands surrounded by severely decidualized stroma. There was evidence of mild acute inflammation on top of chronic inflammation in the periglandular area being observed less commonly in the stroma. (Fig. 1 and Fig. 2).In regards to the medical history of the patient, first of all, there was no evidence as to why the patient had undergone cesarean section in her previous pregnancies, what the causes of abortions were, or when the abortions and the molar pregnancy took place. Secondly, regarding hypothyroidism, the patient had been taking 100 μg of levothyroxine orally as a daily dose except for Fridays when she had been taking 200 μg of levothyroxine.Approximately 16 months after the delivery of the 6th gravidity, the patient was referred to a general surgeon, her chief complaint being abdominal pain at the site of the previous Pfannenstiel incision. Further examinations showed a retractable abdominal mass on the site of the Pfannenstiel incision without any sign of strangulation. The surgeon consequently performed open abdominal hernia (incisional hernia) repair with a mesh graft. A 3 × 3 cm defect on the left lower abdominal wall responsible for the incisional hernia was found. Fig. 3 summarizes the clinical course of the patient.3

Methodology

We searched for “(Endometriosis [tiab] OR endometriotic [tiab] OR endometrioma [tiab]) AND (deciduation [tiab] OR decidualization [tiab] OR decidua [tiab] OR decidualized [tiab]) AND (cesarean [tiab] OR caesarean [tiab] OR surgery [tiab] OR Skin [tiab] OR cutaneous [tiab] OR Pfannenstiel [tiab]) AND (case report)” in PubMed database. Relevant articles were obtained in full text. The bibliographic search ended on February 4th, 2024. The work has been reported in line with the SCARE criteria [16].4

Discussion

Endometriosis is the presence of endometrial tissue outside of the endometrium and myometrium, consisting of endometrial glands and stroma [1]. The classic symptoms associated with this disease are dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain, menstrual irregularities, and infertility [12]. While the exact incidence varies across studies, recent literature suggests that scar endometriosis is a rare complication, occurring in approximately 0.03 % to 2 % of women who have undergone cesarean section [13,14].Approximately 1.3 % of cases of endometriosis are located in the abdominal wall. Despite its rarity, cesarean section scar is, by far, the most common site for abdominal endometriosis (approximately 59 %) [15].Our case report presented a 38-year-old G6P2 woman in which subcutaneous endometriosis was ruled in by ultrasonography and was diagnosed by microscopy. The lesion's size had not grown since the radiologist had evaluated the lesion. Whether it was due to suboptimal healthcare or simply the absence of symptoms, physical examinations of our patient showed nothing problematic. The symptom could have been painless or painful, firm subcutaneous nodule. [[4], [5], [6], [7], [8],10,11]Decidualization of endometriosis can happen under the influence of high progesterone levels. This progesterone can be supplied by either pregnancy or exogenous progesterone consumption. This process would induce atypia (nucleomegaly, prominent nuclei, peripheral margination of chromatin) among decidualized cells to some capacity [5,10]. There are but more differences between decidualized and non-decidualized endometrial tissues. There is evidence that stromal cells are the dominant component [8]. Qualitatively, non-decidualized endometriosis consists of stromal cells that retain their spindle shape and basophilic cytoplasm whereas decimalized endometriosis consists of polygonal stromal cells with abundant amphophilic cytoplasm [6,8].To our knowledge, we present the ninth case report of decidualized endometriosis located on a post-cesarean section scar [[4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], [11]]. Pellegrini et al. [10] were the first to describe such endometriosis. The most important point he made was that pathologists and dermatologists might mistake decidualized endometriosis for malignancy. For instance, Myxoid change is a rare misleading finding in endometriosis that can be misinterpreted as malignancy [4,7,11]. Fortunately, there are some differentiative factors. Atypia and mitotic figures are not characteristics of endometriosis and are seen in malignancies [7,8,10]. Not seeing mitotic figures and atypia is in favor of endometriosis [7]. Contributing factors that may lead clinicians to the diagnosis of malignancy are extrapelvic localization of endometriosis, absence of glands in the tissue, and an elderly non-pregnant patient [4,10].Immunophenotyping alongside microscopy can confirm the diagnosis of endometriosis. CD10 is diffusely present in endometrial stromal cells [4,5]. Knowing the fact that the presentation of CD10 is sensitive but non-specific [5], negative CD10 immunochemistry results can significantly improve the accuracy of our diagnosis.Regarding our case, the patient had not experienced endometriosis before. This lesion was located outside the pelvic cavity. On the other hand, the patient was in the younger demographics, and histopathologically, despite its scarcity, her resected tissue contained glandular structures favoring endometriosis over malignancy. There was no pathologic change in favor of malignancy in our case. Unfortunately, we didn't evaluate the expression of the CD10 marker, while it could help better differentiate the decidualized endometriosis from malignancy.Our case and similar previous cases [[4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], [11]] suggest that mechanical transportation of the endometrium from the uterus to the subcutaneous tissue of the abdominal wall is mostly involved in the pathogenesis [1].In summary, we described a case of post-cesarean section endometriosis in which the presence of this endometriosis was first ruled in, in the second trimester. After delivery, microscopy from resected tissue showed endometrial glands surrounded by severely decidualized stroma. There was evidence of mild acute inflammation on top of chronic inflammation in the periglandular area being observed less commonly in stroma. While our case was in the younger demographics and histopathologically her resected tissue contained glandular structures, no past medical history of endometriosis, extrapelvic localization of the tissue could have led us to a diagnostic pitfall. Precisely differentiating cutaneous decidualized endometriosis from malignancies should be of great importance for dermatologists and pathologists.

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

This study was conducted in full compliance with ethical standards and received approval from the Ethics Committee of Fasa University of Medical Sciences under the ethical code IR.FUMS.REC.1403.059.

Funding

The authors received no financial support for the research, authorship, and/or publication of this article.

Author contribution

Amin Kavari [Writing - Original Draft, Review, and editing].Babak Samizadeh [Conceptualization, Methodology, Data Curation, Project Administration and submission].Maryam Maghbool [Conceptualization, Methodology, Data Curation, and Project Administration].

Guarantor

Maryam Maghbool.

Research registration number

Not applicable.

Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process

During the preparation of this work, the authors only used artificially intelligent proofing tools to enhance the lingual experience for the readers. No other generative AI and AI-assisted technology was used in the writing process. After using the tool, the authors reviewed and edited the content as needed and take full responsibility for the content of the publication.

Conflict of interest statement

None.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 38-year-old woman, G6P2, referred for an elective cesarean section. 
- Chief Complaint: Abdominal pain at the site of the previous Pfannenstiel incision, approximately 16 months postpartum.
- History of Present Illness: Ultrasonography at 16 weeks of gestation revealed a 38 × 29 × 26 mm lobulated solid lesion within the rectus abdominis muscle, midline of the suprapubic abdominal wall. The lesion was resected during the cesarean section.
- Past Medical History: Known hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine.
- Personal History: None reported.
- Family History: None reported.
- Physical Examination: Retractable abdominal mass near the Pfannenstiel incision site during follow-up post-delivery. No evidence of strangulation.
- Ancillary Tests: Ultrasonography revealed a suprapubic lesion. Histopathology of resected tissue showed endometrial glands surrounded by severely decidualized stroma, mild acute inflammation, and chronic inflammation in periglandular areas. Laboratory studies showed normocytic anemia (Hb: 10.1) and mild hypokalemia (K: 3.3). No immunohistochemistry (CD10 evaluation) was performed.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Scar Endometriosis:** Considered based on ultrasonography findings and the history of cesarean section. Further supported by histological evidence of endometrial glands surrounded by decidualized stroma in the resected lesion, consistent with decidualized endometriosis.
- **Malignancy:** Excluded as histopathological analysis showed no atypical mitotic figures or other features consistent with malignancy. The patient’s younger age and the presence of glandular structures favor endometriosis over malignancy.
- **Infectious Entity:** Excluded due to lack of necrotic features or culture-confirming organisms. While inflammation was noted histologically, it was associated with endometriosis rather than an infection.
- Given the extrapelvic localization and decidualized changes, precise differentiation was crucial to rule out malignancy and support endometriosis diagnosis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Pregnancy-related decidualized endometriosis located on a post-cesarean section scar, without evidence of malignancy.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- Patient Information: 38-year-old woman, G6P2, referred for an elective cesarean section. 
- Chief Complaint: Abdominal pain at the site of the previous Pfannenstiel incision, approximately 16 months postpartum.
- History of Present Illness: Ultrasonography at 16 weeks of gestation revealed a 38 × 29 × 26 mm lobulated solid lesion within the rectus abdominis muscle, midline of the suprapubic abdominal wall. The lesion was resected during the cesarean section.
- Past Medical History: Known hypothyroidism managed with levothyroxine.
- Personal History: None reported.
- Family History: None reported.
- Physical Examination: Retractable abdominal mass near the Pfannenstiel incision site during follow-up post-delivery. No evidence of strangulation.
- Ancillary Tests: Ultrasonography revealed a suprapubic lesion. Histopathology of resected tissue showed endometrial glands surrounded by severely decidualized stroma, mild acute inflammation, and chronic inflammation in periglandular areas. Laboratory studies showed normocytic anemia (Hb: 10.1) and mild hypokalemia (K: 3.3). No immunohistochemistry (CD10 evaluation) was performed.

---

results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's case. She's a 38-year-old woman who had a previous Pfannenstiel incision from a cesarean section, and now she has abdominal pain at that site 16 months postpartum. The history mentions that during her 16-week ultrasound, there was a lobulated solid lesion in the rectus abdominis muscle. They decided to remove it during the elective C-section.

First, I need to consider what kind of lesion could develop in the rectus abdominis after a C-section. The Pfannenstiel incision is a midline incision, so the lesion is midline. The fact that it's a solid, lobulated lesion in the muscle might make me think of a few things. Since she had a previous C-section, maybe it's related to scar tissue, but the ultrasound was done 16 weeks before the current surgery, so it's been there for a while.

The histopathology report says endometrial glands with decidualized stroma. Decidualization is a process that happens in the endometrium during pregnancy, so this might suggest a uterine lesion. But the lesion was in the abdominal wall. Wait, that's unusual. Maybe it's a metastasis? But the patient is having an elective C-section, and the lesion was found during the procedure. Alternatively, could this be an endometrioma or some kind of endometrial implant in the abdominal wall?

Wait, the histology shows endometrial glands with decidualized stroma. Decidualization is a sign of a placental reaction, so maybe this is a placental implant or a retained placenta fragment? But that would be more acute, and she's 16 months postpartum. Alternatively, could this be a type of tumor? Maybe a leiomyoma, but the histology mentions endometrial glands, which is more characteristic of endometriosis or a metastasis from the uterus.

But the patient is not known to have any gynecological issues except for hypothyroidism. The lesion was found during pregnancy, and they resected it during the C-section. The presence of endometrial glands with decidualized stroma suggests that the lesion might be a form of endometriosis, specifically an endometrioma, but in the abdominal wall. However, endometriosis typically occurs in the pelvic area, not the abdominal wall. Alternatively, could it be a metastatic endometrial cancer? But the patient is 38, and the lesion was present during pregnancy, which might not align with a cancer diagnosis. Also, the patient had no other symptoms of cancer, like weight loss or abnormal bleeding.

Another possibility is a gestational trophoblastic disease, but that usually presents with different symptoms and would have more specific markers. The presence of decidual stroma could be a sign of a placental site tumor, but that's rare. Alternatively, maybe the lesion is a benign endometrial implant that was present from a previous pregnancy, but why would it be in the abdominal wall?

Wait, the lesion was in the rectus abdominis muscle, midline. The Pfannenstiel incision is a horizontal incision in the lower abdomen, so the rectus muscle is there. Maybe the lesion was a result of an endometrial implant that somehow migrated into the abdominal wall during a previous surgery, like a C-section. But that's a stretch. Alternatively, could this be a case of a myoma that's in the abdominal wall? But the histology shows endometrial glands, not smooth muscle.

Another angle: the patient had a previous C-section, and during pregnancy, the ultrasound found the lesion. The fact that the lesion was resected during the current C-section might be because they thought it was a benign lesion. The histology shows endometrial glands with decidualization, which might be a sign of a placental implant. However, 16 months postpartum, a placental implant would have been resolved by now. Unless it's a persistent placental site lesion, but that's more of a rare condition.

Wait, the histology also mentions mild acute and chronic inflammation. The patient has normocytic anemia and mild hypokalemia. The anemia could be due to chronic inflammation, but that's not specific. The hypokalemia might be due to some other issue, like diuretic use or vomiting, but the patient's history doesn't mention that.

Putting it all together: the lesion in the abdominal wall with endometrial glands and decidual stroma is most likely an endometrioid lesion. However, endometriosis in the abdominal wall is very rare. Another possibility is a metastatic endometrial cancer, but the timing doesn't fit. Alternatively, could this be a benign endometrial implant that developed after a previous pregnancy, perhaps from a surgical procedure? Maybe a hematoma or a scar tissue with endometrial cells? Or perhaps the lesion is a form of a desmoid tumor, but that's usually fibrous and not with endometrial glands.

Wait, the presence of decidualized stroma is a key point. Decidualization is a response to hormonal changes, especially during pregnancy. So if the lesion was present during pregnancy, maybe it's a placental implant that's persistent. However, placental implants typically occur at the site of placental attachment, which is usually in the uterus. But if there was a placental implant in the abdominal wall, that would be very unusual. Alternatively, maybe it's a type of endometrioma that's been there since a previous pregnancy, but again, in the abdominal wall is rare.

Another thought: the lesion could be a granuloma, but the histology shows endometrial glands. The combination of endometrial glands and decidual stroma is more specific for endometriosis or endometrioid tumors. However, endometriosis in the abdominal wall is called abdominal wall endometriosis, which is a rare form. It can occur after surgeries, especially C-sections, where endometrial cells might have been implanted into the abdominal wall during the procedure. This would explain the presence of endometrial glands and decidual stroma, as the tissue is responding to hormonal changes, causing cyclical pain and swelling. The patient's abdominal pain at the site of the incision 16 months postpartum could be due to this.

So the final diagnosis would be abdominal wall endometriosis, with the lesion being a result of endometrial implantation during a previous C-section. The histology supports this with the presence of endometrial glands and decidual stroma. The anemia and hypokalemia might be unrelated, perhaps due to other factors like diet or medication.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient's history reveals a lobulated solid lesion in the rectus abdominis muscle, detected during pregnancy and resected during a cesarean section. The lesion's location at the Pfannenstiel incision site raises suspicion of an abdominal wall pathology.  
<step 2> Histopathology shows endometrial glands surrounded by decidualized stroma, a hallmark of endometriosis. Decidualization is a response to hormonal stimulation, commonly seen in endometriotic implants.  
<step 3> The presence of endometrial tissue in the abdominal wall, along with the patient's postpartum timeline, strongly suggests endometriosis secondary to implantation during a prior cesarean section. This is a recognized cause of abdominal wall endometriosis.  
<step 4> The absence of immunohistochemistry (e.g., CD10) limits definitive confirmation, but the histologic findings align with endometriosis. The anemia and hypokalemia are likely unrelated, possibly due to chronic inflammation or other non-specific factors.  
<step 5> Differential diagnoses such as endometrial metastasis or placental implantation are less likely given the timeline and lack of malignant features in the histology.  

### Answer:
Abdominal wall endometriosis.
